title: Listening - Part 4: Running Astronomy Evenings
description: You will hear part of a radio interview with a man called Daniel, who runs public astronomy sessions at an observatory. For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

content.audio_script: Presenter: Today we’re at Hilltop Observatory with Daniel Ortiz, who runs evening astronomy sessions for the public. Daniel, what first attracted you to the night sky? || Daniel: My parents weren’t scientists, and we lived in a city where few stars were visible. When I was fourteen, we stayed on a farm during a family holiday. [24]I stepped outside after dark and was astonished to see the Milky Way clearly for the first time[/24]. I wanted to know what I was looking at, so I borrowed astronomy books from the library when we returned. || Presenter: Did you always intend to work in an observatory? || Daniel: I studied physics, but research was not quite right for me because I missed contact with people. I volunteered during a public open night here and helped visitors find Saturn through a telescope. [25]Their excitement when they saw its rings convinced me that sharing astronomy was the work I wanted to do[/25]. The observatory later created an education post. || Presenter: What difficulty do beginners have when using a telescope? || Daniel: People often expect a giant, colourful image like a photograph from a space telescope. Through an ordinary instrument, objects may look smaller and paler. [26]The challenge is helping visitors adjust their expectations so they notice the remarkable detail that is genuinely visible[/26]. Once they know what to look for, they are rarely disappointed. || Presenter: Does cloudy weather ruin your events? || Daniel: It changes them. We cannot promise clear skies, so I always prepare indoor activities. Last winter a family arrived after travelling a long way and clouds covered everything. [27]We used a computer model to explore the planets, and the children became so absorbed that they forgot they had not looked through a telescope[/27]. A good astronomy evening is about curiosity, not only observation. || Presenter: Is light pollution a major concern? || Daniel: Yes. Excessive lighting makes stars harder to see and can affect wildlife. I used to think the only solution was turning off street lighting, which people resist for safety reasons. [28]Now we work with councils to fit lights that point downwards, improving visibility on the ground while reducing light sent into the sky[/28]. It is a practical compromise. || Presenter: Have phone apps changed stargazing? || Daniel: They can be very helpful for locating a planet or constellation. But if visitors keep watching the screen, they miss the actual experience. [29]I encourage people to use an app briefly for direction, then put the phone away and let their eyes adapt to darkness[/29]. Seeing more stars takes patience. || Presenter: What can someone do without a telescope? || Daniel: Much more than people think. [30]Begin by learning a few easy constellations with your own eyes, because they provide a map for finding other objects later[/30]. Expensive equipment is not the starting point; familiarity with the sky is.

task.title: Part 4
task.instruction: For questions 24–30, choose the correct answer (A, B or C).

questions.question: What first stimulated Daniel's interest in astronomy?

options.A: A lesson taught by a scientist at school.
options.B: Seeing a very clear night sky on holiday.
options.C: Receiving a telescope from his parents.

questions.explanation: During a rural holiday, he first saw the Milky Way clearly.

questions.question: Why did Daniel choose education work at an observatory?

options.A: He enjoyed people's reactions when viewing Saturn.
options.B: He found university physics too difficult.
options.C: An observatory offered him a research project.

questions.explanation: Helping visitors see Saturn's rings showed him he wanted to share astronomy.

questions.question: What challenge does Daniel mention when working with beginners?

options.A: Teaching them to operate complicated telescopes safely.
options.B: Persuading them to attend events in cold weather.
options.C: Helping them appreciate what telescopes can realistically show.

questions.explanation: Visitors may expect photographic images and need help noticing real visible details.

questions.question: What does Daniel say about an event affected by clouds?

options.A: The visitors enjoyed an alternative indoor activity.
options.B: The observatory returned the family's money.
options.C: The children asked to attend on another night.

questions.explanation: The computer model kept the children fully interested despite the clouds.

questions.question: How is Daniel's observatory helping to reduce light pollution?

options.A: By requesting that street lights be removed completely.
options.B: By promoting lighting that directs light towards the ground.
options.C: By closing the observatory during certain hours.

questions.explanation: Downward-directed lights preserve ground visibility while reducing sky glow.

questions.question: What does Daniel advise about stargazing apps?

options.A: Use them briefly, then focus on the real sky.
options.B: Avoid them because they show inaccurate positions.
options.C: Keep them on while looking through a telescope.

questions.explanation: He suggests using an app for direction only, then allowing eyes to adapt.

questions.question: What does Daniel recommend for someone starting astronomy?

options.A: Buy a telescope designed for beginners.
options.C: Learn basic star patterns without equipment.

questions.explanation: He recommends first recognising simple constellations with the naked eye.
